story:
Leah was lonely—not dramatic about it, just quietly aware of the empty places in her life, especially at this festive time of year. Work kept her occupied; weekends stretched. One mild December Saturday, with her small flat already cleaned and her bank balance too low for shopping, she decided to walk in the nearby park she had never quite dared to enter alone. At first it was busy with buggies and dogs; then the paths thinned, the air turned hush-soft under winter trees, and she let her thoughts drift to the old days—friends, parties, a life that had slipped forward while she stood still. Without noticing, she wandered past where most people walked.

Footsteps came up behind her. A young man, alone. He looked ordinary, yet the look in his eyes told her he wasn’t just a passerby, not even a thief content with a phone and purse. Panic rose. No one else was around. By the time she understood the danger, it was already too late.

That evening, Mark Ross hurried through the same park with his dog, Rufus, hoping for a quick loop before a night out. He threw a stick, whistled, and cursed when the dog didn’t return. When he found Rufus, the dog stood over a woman lying in the leaves, her coat and hands dark with blood. Mark called for help, then knelt beside her and kept talking—his voice low, steady—telling her to hold on, that help was coming, that she wasn’t alone. He stayed until the ambulance took her away.

Leah woke under hospital lights to pain and the sense that the attack was still happening, her body braced for it. She remembered the knife, the weight pinning her, how she had tried to fight and pleaded, how the minutes had stretched and blurred. She also remembered a different voice, close to her ear, coaxing her back. A policewoman sat outside her door. At first Leah was too sedated to tell the story. Later, when she was stronger, the officers told her she was lucky: the blade had missed her vital organs. There would be scars and nightmares, but she was alive. They had a suspect—a previously convicted rapist on parole who had no alibi. When she was ready, she would have to identify him.

Mark came back to the hospital the next day, then the day after, not entirely sure why except that he needed to know she would be all right. When Leah could manage visitors, they spoke about everything except the attack. They discovered they lived close by, knew some of the same people, and shared the same quiet sense of having been left behind as others paired off and moved on. When she was discharged and afraid to sleep alone, Mark offered to stay on her sofa. He did not make a fuss of it, he simply showed up with a blanket and his calm, and it helped.

In time, Leah picked the man from a lineup. The investigation moved forward, and life—stubbornly, imperfectly—did too. She and Mark started walking Rufus together, then cooking together, then staying. What had begun in crisis became, slowly, something ordinary and tender.

Two years later, they walked through a different park, far from the one they had left behind. Joshua, their three-month-old son, slept in the pram. They had Saturday plans—shopping, a coffee, nothing special—and it felt like everything.

The man who attacked her, Michael Fischer, was identified, tried, and sentenced to eight years for sexual assault and attempted murder. He died by suicide in prison at twenty-seven.
